text: Chapter 8 applies harmonic substitution and voice-leading principles to the jazz standard On Green Dolphin Street, a tune built around the signature move of replacing the I chord with its i minor relative in bar 3—a substitution pattern the book cross-references from earlier chapters. The chapter introduces tritone substitution (a chord replaced by another with a root a tritone away) and chromatic passing chords, then teaches the minimal-change voice-leading principle: when improvising or arranging over confusing harmonic changes, keep it simple by asking "what are the defining notes of each chord?" and adjusting only the intervals (especially 3rds and 7ths) that carry the chord quality. Key load-bearing concepts include the minor-third key-center shift as a recurring compositional pattern, the defining-notes question as an analytical tool for any harmonically dense passage, motif (a short rhythmic or melodic passage repeated throughout a composition), chromatic passing tones (non-diatonic half-step connectors), and the explicit permission to use extensions (9ths, 11ths, 13ths) over 7th chords. The chapter governs improvisation strategy with the principle of simplicity over crowding, and offers a concrete practice technique—playing as few notes as possible, sometimes just one note over an entire sequence—to isolate chord-tone hearing in the practice room.
